summary: DHH argues that code style matters deeply and that Ruby's expressive syntax is its greatest strength. He pushes back against the idea that enforced, universal linting—like Go's built-in formatter—is desirable, comparing it to Orwell's Newspeak. He views code as a form of literary expression where individual style should flourish, not be flattened. Rails will ship with RuboCop and his own default style preferences, but explicitly as a starting point, not a mandate. The post positions Ruby's multiple ways of expressing the same logic as a feature, not a flaw, contrasting it with Python's 'one obvious way' philosophy.
key_points: Universal, language-enforced code formatting (like Go's gofmt) is too totalitarian and kills expressive style
Code is a form of prose and Ruby's rich syntax makes it uniquely suited for 'writer-programmers'
Linters are valuable when teams choose their own style, not when style is imposed from above
Rails will ship RuboCop with DHH's default preferences as a starting point, not a permanent edict
Ruby's 'many ways to do things' philosophy is the opposite of Python's and is a strength
money_quotes: Imagine every novel written in the same style, Hemingway indistinguishable from Dickens, Tolkien from Rowling. It would be awfully gray to enjoy the English language if there was only a single shade of prose.
The best code to me is indeed its own form of poetry, and style is an integral part of such expression. And in no language more so than Ruby.
There are many ways to do almost everything, and it's why reading and writing Ruby is such a delight to people like me who strive to write code as an author more often than as an engineer.
That to me is the essence of style, it's invariably idiosyncratic. We wouldn't even be talking about style if it was all the same for everyone all the time.
Ruby affords us such rich and expressive syntax that it would be a damn shame if we narrowed all possible paths down to a single, pre-approved trail. Ruby is for writers.
I think they're excellent tools for authors and teams to enforce their style with consistency on their codebases.
key_insight: Code style is a form of literary expression, and Ruby's multiple ways of doing things make it uniquely suited for programmers who think of themselves as writers rather than engineers.
